title: Add zizmorcore/zizmor-action to GitHub allow-list and ADR-002
description:
## Why

ADR-002 requires that adding a new action includes both pinning it AND adding it to the GitHub repository allow-list. zizmorcore/zizmor-action was added to pinning-lint.yml (SHA-pinned correctly as Tier A) but was not added to the allow-list in repository settings or to the allow-list section in ADR-002.

## What needs to be done

1. Add zizmorcore/zizmor-action@* to repository Settings → Actions → General → Allow select actions and reusable workflows
2. Add zizmorcore/zizmor-action@* to the Current entries list in the ADR-002 allow-list section (docs/architecture/decisions/002-github-actions-pinning-policy.md)

## Acceptance criteria

- zizmorcore/zizmor-action@* present in GitHub repository allow-list settings
- ADR-002 Current entries list includes zizmorcore/zizmor-action@*
- Both changes consistent with each other

title: Fix allow-list entries in ADR-002: add trailing commas
description: ADR-002 § Allow-List Configuration lists allow-list entries one per line without commas. This implies newlines work as separators in GitHub's Settings → Actions allow-list textbox, but they do not — GitHub requires comma-separated entries. Anyone copying the code block verbatim will get a broken allow-list. Fix: add trailing comma to each entry line in the code block.

title: Add prescriptive per-action allow-list MUST to constitution Principle IX rule 4
description: Constitution Principle IX rule 4 currently says 'Each repository instance MUST enable the allow-list per ADR-002 § Allow-List Configuration' (fork setup) and 'See ADR-002 for full criteria' (soft cross-reference). An agent adding a new action may correctly apply the pinning tier rules and stop without following the soft reference to find the allow-list update step. Add a prescriptive sentence: 'When adding a new action to a workflow, agents MUST also add owner/repo@* to the repository allow-list per ADR-002 § Allow-List Configuration.' This closes the gap between a fork-setup MUST and a per-action-addition MUST.

title: Finding 5: Add 'no recurring out-of-pocket cost' as ADR-002 decision driver
description:
ADR-002 Decision Drivers do not currently capture an operational constraint the maintainer wants explicit: this is an open-source personal repository and the maintainer does not want to incur recurring monetary cost to maintain the pinning policy. This is a non-trivial constraint that should weigh in option selection — specifically:

- Option F (buy hardening product, e.g. Harden-Runner) — free tier is acceptable, paid tier is not
- Option G (repository allow-list) — must remain on GitHub Free tier
- Any tooling (pinact, ratchet, zizmor) chosen for the follow-up CI lint — must be free / OSS, no paid SaaS

Fix: add a new decision driver to ADR-002 (provisional name 'D5 — No recurring out-of-pocket cost'), priority somewhere between D2 (auditability) and D3 (patch velocity), and re-test each option against it. Update pros/cons where the driver flips a verdict (most relevantly Option F).

Acceptance:
- New decision driver added with one-sentence rationale referencing the maintainer's open-source posture
- Each option's pros/cons cross-reference D5 where the option has a recurring-cost dimension
- Recommendation paragraph notes whether D5 changed the verdict
- Option F pros/cons explicitly tag paid-tier features as 'D5-violating, defer adoption'

title: Finding 4: Re-evaluate ADR-002 recommendation against mitigations + threat IDs
description:
ADR-002 Mitigations sections currently sit below pros/cons and read as per-option apologetics rather than load-bearing inputs to the Recommendation. After Findings 1, 2, 3 land, re-walk every option through:
  (a) the explicit threat IDs added in Finding 3, and
  (b) each option's mitigations

and test whether the Recommendation still stands as bare Option A.

Specific cases worth re-evaluating:
- Option B + auto-merge for actions/* SHA bumps + pinact tooling: D3 (patch velocity) gap closes substantially. Residual delta = trust in auto-merge. If acceptable, B becomes competitive (simpler rule, uniform SHA, no Tier B identity-trust dependency).
- Option C + Option F (Harden-Runner): runtime egress monitoring catches what tag-retargeting opens up. Worse on auditability (D2). Possibly acceptable for this repo's blast radius.
- Option A + Option G (allow-list): ADR already names this combo but does not commit to it as the recommendation. If recommended, recommendation should be 'A+G', not bare A.

Method: after Findings 1, 2, 3 land, do the option-vs-threat-vs-mitigation matrix walk. Update Recommendation accordingly. If no shift, document why explicitly.

Meta-fix: the Mitigations sections should be positioned in the ADR such that the reader cannot read pros/cons without already knowing the mitigations. (Note: skill-level fix tracked separately as Finding 6.)

Acceptance:
- Recommendation paragraph cites threat IDs and mitigations explicitly
- Each rejected option includes a one-sentence 'rejected even with mitigations because ...' justification
- If Recommendation shifts (e.g. to A+G or B+auto-merge), Sync Impact and follow-up tasks updated accordingly

title: Finding 3: Add explicit threat model section to ADR-002
description:
ADR-002 currently jumps from mixed-pinning observation to mechanism (Tier A/B) without enumerating the threats the policy is supposed to address. A decision-maker has to reverse-engineer the threat model from the pros/cons.

Fix: insert an explicit Threat Model section between Context and Decision Drivers. Enumerate threats and, for each, note in scope vs out of scope and whether the recommended option addresses it.

Candidate threats to discuss:
1. Pipeline credential leak — GHCR push token + OIDC ID token exposed to malicious action code running in the push job. (Highest blast radius.)
2. Malicious artefact published — image carrying backdoor under our signed identity; cosign signature still valid because we sign whatever we built. Downstream trust chain weaponized.
3. CI secret exfil — Ansible Vault password, SSH keys, cloud creds leaked via secrets.* env exposure to action code. (Currently low-surface in this repo's CI but framing should not assume that.)
4. Supply chain to maintainer dev box — compromised action writes malicious content into .devcontainer/ that gets pulled to local laptops. Indirect attack path.
5. Phishing / social-engineered PRs — adversary opens PR adding 'uses: evil/foo@v1' betting on reviewer fatigue. Policy + lint reduces blast radius.
6. Bot-net abuse of forks — fork CI as crypto miner / proxy. Likely out of scope; name and dismiss.

After enumeration: map each Considered Option to which threats it materially reduces. Reader should be able to see at a glance why Option A is recommended for the threat profile of this repo.

Acceptance:
- New Threat Model section exists between Context and Decision Drivers
- Each threat states: in/out of scope, owning option, severity for this repo
- Pros/cons of each option cross-reference threat IDs
- Recommendation paragraph cites threat IDs explicitly

Note: this finding pairs with Finding 4 (re-evaluate mitigations against threats). Once threats are explicit, the right mitigations become testable. Keep this finding scoped to the threat-model section; Finding 4 handles the solution re-evaluation.

title: Finding 2: Forbid beads-ID refs in durable artefacts; add constitution principle + sweep existing refs
description:
Beads issue IDs are ephemeral — closed issues get purged with no archive of descriptions. Any code/doc/ADR/constitution reference to a beads ID becomes a dead string the moment the issue closes, leaving the reader with no way to recover what the reference meant.

Current ADR-002 itself contains such refs: urf, v2u, i3p, 86r appear inline in the Confirmation, Enforcement, More Information, and Mitigation sections.

Two-part fix:

1. Constitution amendment (MINOR bump 1.7.0 → 1.8.0): add a new principle (provisional name: 'No External-System References in Durable Artefacts') stating that durable artefacts (code, Markdown docs, ADRs, constitution, YAML config) MUST NOT reference beads issue IDs or any other ephemeral external-tracker ID. Substance MUST be inlined. Beads refs remain allowed in PR descriptions and commit message bodies (which become immutable git history once committed) and in .omc/ working files.

2. Sweep: remove beads-ID refs from existing durable artefacts. Specifically:
   - ADR-002 — replace urf/v2u/i3p/86r with inline descriptions ('the follow-up CI lint', 'the source finding', 'the curl|sudo finding', etc.)
   - Any other ADR or doc containing similar refs (audit needed)
   - AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md beads sections — review and amend

Acceptance:
- Constitution amended with new principle, Sync Impact Report updated, version bumped 1.7.0 → 1.8.0
- All ADRs and docs in docs/ free of beads-ID refs
- AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md beads sections do not encourage placing beads IDs in durable artefacts
close_reason: split into Finding 2a (ipj — constitution principle) and Finding 2b (bq7 — sweep after F4)

title: Replace curl|sudo mv of container-structure-test /latest/ binary in CI
description:
Code-review finding from ADR-002 reviews. docker-publish.yml test job (lines ~107-110) downloads container-structure-test from a floating /latest/ download URL and sudo-mv's it into /usr/local/bin/ on every run, with no checksum or signature verification. The test job is needs: of the push job which holds packages:write + id-token:write — so a compromised /latest/ binary executes inside the credential path of the publish pipeline.

This is independent of the pinning policy (ADR-002). It is a separate supply-chain finding that ADR-002 explicitly lists as out of scope (and references this issue).

Acceptance:
- container-structure-test installed via pinned release tag (not /latest/)
- Either: SHA256 checksum verified against an upstream-published value, OR cosign verify of the released artefact if upstream signs releases
- No floating-version downloads in any job that is a needs: ancestor of the push job
- Document chosen approach in a short note inside docker-publish.yml header

Out of scope: switching tool, replacing with a GitHub Action wrapper (separate eval).

title: Add CI lint job to enforce GitHub Actions pinning policy
description:
Enforce the two-tier pinning policy defined in ADR 002 (follow-up from finding urf). Add a workflow that fails the build when Tier A actions (third-party: docker/*, sigstore/*, anything with packages:write / id-token:write / signing / registry push) are pinned by tag instead of 40-char SHA. Tier B (first-party actions/* utilities) may keep floating major tags.

Tool evaluation REQUIRED before selection — assess upstream activity and health for each candidate via GitHub MCP or gh CLI (last release, commit cadence, open-issue trend, maintainer count, archived flag, CVE history):

- zizmor (Rust, built-in unpinned-uses audit)
- pinact (auto-pins + --check mode)
- ratchet (Go, ratchet check)

Record findings + chosen tool + rejection rationale in design field. Fallback: hand-rolled regex grep if all candidates fail health check.

Acceptance:
- Tool selected with documented health-check evidence
- Lint runs in CI on push + PR
- Tier A tag-pin in any workflow fails the job
- Tier B tag-pin passes
- Allow-list mechanism for documented exceptions (comment-based or config)
- README / ADR 002 cross-link to the lint job

Out of scope: rewriting existing pins (already conformant), changing the policy itself.
design: Tool health check (2026-05-21): zizmor (woodruffw/zizmor) v1.25.2 — 5182 stars, pushed 2026-05-20, not archived, most active project; pinact (suzuki-shunsuke/pinact) v3.10.1 — 1020 stars, pushed 2026-05-21, not archived, active but lacks native Tier A/B policy layer; ratchet (sethvargo/ratchet) v0.11.4 — 935 stars, last release 2025-06-27 (~11 months), slower cadence. Selected: zizmor. Rejection rationale — pinact: check mode flags all non-SHA pins uniformly, no built-in ref-pin policy for actions/* Tier B; ratchet: 11-month release gap indicates slower maintenance. zizmor provides native unpinned-uses.config.policies supporting hash-pin vs ref-pin per pattern, directly encoding the two-tier policy without wrappers. Config: .github/zizmor.yml. Workflow: .github/workflows/pinning-lint.yml. Verified: conformant workflows exit 0; docker/login-action@v3 (Tier A tag-pin) exits 14 with high-severity unpinned-uses error; actions/checkout@v6 (Tier B) exits 0.

title: Finding 2: Establish GitHub Actions pinning policy (SHA vs version tags)
description: Audit current pinning strategy in .github/workflows/docker-publish.yml. Currently mixed: SHA pins on docker/*, sigstore/* (artifact/credential actions) vs version tags on checkout, upload/download-artifact (utility actions). Create explicit policy document specifying which action categories warrant SHA pinning vs which can use version tags with Dependabot auto-patching. Rationale: SHAs prevent tag retagging attacks (immutable), version tags enable faster security patch velocity via Dependabot. Trade-off: readability vs maintainability vs attack surface. Aligns with Principle IX (CI/CD Pipeline Security) — explicit, auditable artifact provenance chain.
close_reason: ADR-002 accepted. Pinning policy established as A+G (two-tier policy + GitHub allow-list). This finding is resolved.

title: Refine docker-publish.yml: build → test → push order
description:
## Why

After ngu implements fork-safe artifact passing, the non-PR path (push to main/tags) still pushes to GHCR before running tests. This means a broken image can land in the registry.

## What needs to be done

Reorganize docker-publish.yml so the pipeline enforces build → test → push on ALL paths:

1. Build job: build image and export as tarball artifact (already done for PR path via ngu)
2. Test job: download artifact, load image, run container-structure-test — same job for both PR and non-PR paths
3. Push job: only runs after test job succeeds AND event is not a pull_request; pulls tarball artifact, loads image, pushes to GHCR, signs with cosign

This ensures GHCR only ever receives images that have passed structure tests.

## Acceptance criteria

- On PR: build → test → no push (same as ngu outcome)
- On push to main/tag: build → test → push (push only if test passes)
- No image ever reaches GHCR without a passing test job
- Cosign signing still runs after push
close_reason: build→test→push enforced on all paths. PR #6 merged to eudicy/ansible-all-my-things main. Run 25940172292 confirms: build×2, test×2, push×2 all pass. latest+latest-amd64 pushed and cosign-signed (sha256:578f41ef...). latest-arm64 pushed by arm64 leg.

title: Security: Disable Administrator RDP login
description:
## Why

Leaving Administrator RDP access enabled is a security risk — direct admin login over RDP is a common attack vector.

## What

Disable RDP login for the Administrator account during provisioning.

## Open Question

Need to determine how to perform GUI operations that require Administrator after disabling RDP for that account. Hypothesis: a normal user with admin group membership can re-enable Administrator RDP access via elevated command prompt when needed.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Administrator account cannot log in via RDP after provisioning
- A documented procedure exists for temporarily re-enabling Administrator RDP access when required for GUI operations
- Provisioning step is idempotent
notes:
## References

- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/windows-security/access-computer-after-administrator-disabled
- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/manufacture/desktop/enable-and-disable-the-built-in-administrator-account?view=windows-11

## Key Finding

Enable/disable built-in Administrator account via elevated command prompt:

```cmd
net user administrator /active:yes
net user administrator /active:no
```

Normal user with admin privileges can run this to temporarily re-enable Administrator RDP access when needed for GUI operations.

title: Security: Install Windows updates during provisioning
description:
## Why

Freshly provisioned Windows instances may have unpatched vulnerabilities. Updates must be applied as early as provisioning allows to reduce attack surface.

## What

Add a provisioning step that installs all pending Windows updates on the host.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Windows updates installed automatically during provisioning
- Provisioning fails or warns if updates cannot be applied
- Step runs early enough in provisioning sequence to minimize exposure window

title: Reduce Docker image size with multi-stage build
description:
## Why

Current .devcontainer/Dockerfile uses a single stage — build tools (pip,
pipx, git, python3-venv, gcc etc.) end up in the runtime image, inflating
size unnecessarily. Large images slow CI pull times and increase storage cost.

## Solution

Adopt multi-stage build pattern from BadWinniePooh/ansible-collection
(.docker/Dockerfile, sha b51cf5f):

- **builder stage**: full toolchain — python3, pip, pipx, git, openssh-client.
  Installs ansible-core via pipx, runs pip install -r requirements.txt,
  runs ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml.
- **runtime stage**: only python3 + openssh-client apt packages.
  Copy /root/.local (pipx venv + wrappers) and /root/.ansible (collections)
  from builder. No compilers, no pip, no pipx binary, no git in final image.

## Acceptance criteria

- .devcontainer/Dockerfile uses FROM ... AS builder + second FROM for runtime
- Final image contains ansible-playbook, ansible-galaxy executables
- Final image does NOT contain pip, pipx, git binaries
- container-structure-test suite passes on the new image
- Image size reduced vs. current single-stage build (document before/after in commit)
notes:
ubuntu:26.04 adopted for both stages. Key findings:
1. gpg-agent must be explicitly installed in ubuntu builder (not auto-dep of gpg unlike Debian)
2. AWS CLI PyInstaller binary uses $ORIGIN RPATH — when COPY breaks the symlink chain (from /usr/local/bin/aws into dist/), the bootloader looks for libpython3.14.so.1.0 in /usr/local/bin/ and fails. Fix: symlink /usr/local/bin/aws -> /usr/local/aws-cli/v2/current/bin/aws so bootloader resolves the real path inside dist/ where the bundled library lives.
3. locales-all dropped (was 300MB uncompressed); ubuntu base has locales package sufficient for ansible.
4. Final image: 686 MB. Tests: 6/6 pass.
5. container-structure-test needs Podman socket active: systemctl --user start podman.socket
close_reason: ubuntu:26.04 base adopted; all 6 container-structure-tests pass; image 686 MB

title: Fork-safe Docker CI: fix docker-publish.yml for fork compatibility
description:
## Why

docker-publish.yml hardcodes 'wonderbird/ansible-toolchain' as image name. On forks:
- Push steps fail (read-only GITHUB_TOKEN)
- Test jobs validate wrong image (upstream :latest, not the built one)
- Both architectures untested on PRs

Breaks CI for all contributors working on forks.

## What needs to be done

Implement the design from docs/features/fork-safe-docker-ci/:

1. Replace hardcoded image name with `${{ github.repository_owner }}/ansible-toolchain`
2. Split build into per-arch matrix: ubuntu-24.04 (amd64) + ubuntu-24.04-arm (arm64)
3. PR path: build → docker save tarball → upload artifact → test job downloads + loads
4. Non-PR path: push to ghcr.io → test job pulls from registry
5. Scope GHA cache keys per arch
6. Guard cosign/push steps with `github.event_name != 'pull_request'`

## Acceptance criteria

- PR from any fork: both archs build and test with no registry push
- Push to main: images published to fork owner's ghcr.io namespace
- No hardcoded 'wonderbird' in workflow file

title: Add pipeline job to check tracked tool version pins
description: query-versions.yml can detect stale version pins but is only run manually. Add a scheduled pipeline job (e.g. weekly GitHub Actions workflow) that runs query-versions.yml and reports or opens a PR when any pin is stale. Relates to the planned GitHub Actions integration noted in docs/architecture/version-update-playbooks.md Outlook.

title: Add no-version-history rule to constitution
description: Constitution should state that version history (changelogs, amendment logs) must not be added to rules files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, skill SKILL.md files). Exception: the constitution itself tracks versions per its governance section. Prevents rules files from accumulating stale history noise.

title: Constitution: add Fail-Loud principle (no silent skip / no empty fact fallthrough)
description:
Elevate the fail-loud NFR (currently spec.md FR-012 in feature 007-version-update-playbooks) into a cross-cutting constitution principle.

Decision (2026-05-12): Failure modes that silently skip work, return empty values, or proceed with unset/None facts are prohibited. Any task that fetches, parses, or computes a required value MUST fail with an explicit error identifying the source when the value cannot be obtained.

Scope to think through:
- Wording of the principle (e.g. Principle VIII: Fail Loud)
- MUST/SHOULD scope: applies to all roles, playbooks, task files
- Relationship to existing Principle I (Idempotency): fail-loud is orthogonal but reinforces predictability
- Version bump: MINOR (new principle)
- Sync impact: AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, templates, possibly fix-problem skill
- Once added: spec.md FR-012 in feature 007 can reference the principle instead of restating; per-task notes in tasks.md may simplify

Trigger: surfaced during /speckit.analyze on feature 007-version-update-playbooks where 3 of 4 fetch tasks lacked explicit parse-failure handling. User decision: not a feature-local concern — belongs in constitution.

title: Align documentation structure: technical vs product/feature split
description:
Define and apply consistent documentation taxonomy across the repo.

Decision (2026-05-12): technical concepts belong in docs/architecture/. Business logic and product aspects belong in feature/product documentation (structure TBD).

Scope to think through:
- What lives in docs/architecture/ (technical concepts, decisions, technical-debt, solution-strategy)
- What lives in docs/improvements/ today and whether it should move
- Where business-logic / product-aspect documentation goes (folder name + structure)
- Migration policy for existing docs that mix concerns
- Update review-documentation-here skill to encode the taxonomy

Trigger: surfaced during /speckit.analyze on feature 007-version-update-playbooks where spec.md and plan.md disagreed on concept-doc placement.

title: Make avoid-duplication principle explicit in constitution
description: The constitution references 'without duplicating logic' implicitly but does not state DRY/avoid-duplication as a named principle. Add it explicitly so agents and contributors have a clear mandate.

title: Verify MCP settings are backed up and restored correctly
description: wc0 fixed backup/restore exclusions but never confirmed what 'MCPs not backed up properly' meant. Need to: (1) identify where MCP server configs live on lorien (global settings.json vs per-project .mcp.json), (2) run a test backup+restore cycle, (3) confirm MCP configs survive round-trip.

title: Fix Claude config backup/restore (MCPs, HUD)
description: Claude config backup incomplete — MCPs and HUD settings not backed up/restored properly. Reference guides: https://github.com/jtklinger/claude-code-backup-guide and https://dirkstrauss.com/claude-code-windows-migration-guide/ — crawl these to identify what files/dirs need backing up. Fix backup process to include all necessary config.

title: Finding: molecule prepare fails on ARM64 due to apt clock skew
description:
## Why

Discovered while running molecule test for claude_code role on ARM64 host. The prepare.yml apt-get update fails with:

  E: Release file ... is not valid yet (invalid for another 10h ...)

The Podman container clock is behind the APT mirror timestamp. prepare.yml uses bare apt-get update with no clock-skew tolerance flag.

## What needs to be done

Add -o Acquire::Check-Valid-Until=false or Acquire::Max-FutureTime=86400 to the apt-get update raw command in prepare.yml to tolerate clock skew in ARM64 Podman containers.

## Acceptance criteria

- molecule test runs past prepare phase on ARM64
- apt-get update does not fail due to future-dated release files

title: Clone beads repo to target VM for expert consultation
description:
## Why

We consult oh-my-claudecode skills on the VM by cloning its repo. We need the same capability for beads — clone the beads repository so a beads expert agent can be invoked locally on the VM, consulting beads docs/skills just as we do with oh-my-claudecode.

## What needs to be done

1. Add an Ansible task (likely in a dev-tools or similar role) to clone https://github.com/gastownhall/beads.git to the target VM
2. Choose install path consistent with other tool repos on the VM (e.g. ~/tools/beads or /opt/beads)
3. Ensure the clone is kept up-to-date (idempotent git clone or pull task)

## Acceptance criteria

- beads repo present on target VM at chosen path
- Task is idempotent (re-run safe)
- Path documented or discoverable for agent use

title: Fix unresolved merge conflict in ADR-002
description:
## Why

ADR-002 (docs/architecture/decisions/002-github-actions-pinning-policy.md) contains unresolved git merge conflict markers from the v2u branch merge (lines 755-766). The file is malformed and the conflict must be resolved.

## What needs to be done

Resolve the conflict in follow-up task 6 of ADR-002: keep the v2u version (Done: zizmor-based CI lint implemented) and remove the HEAD version and conflict markers.

## Acceptance criteria

- ADR-002 contains no conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>)
- Follow-up task 6 correctly reflects that the zizmor CI lint is implemented (Done state)

title: Add scheduled workflow to detect new container-structure-test releases
description:
## Why

CST is pinned via curl in a run: step — Dependabot cannot track it. i3p pinned the version and SHA256 correctly but left no automated update signal. A new CST release will go unnoticed until someone manually checks.

## What needs to be done

Add a scheduled GitHub Actions workflow (e.g. .github/workflows/cst-version-check.yml) that:
1. Queries GitHub releases API for latest GoogleContainerTools/container-structure-test release
2. Compares against the pinned CST_VERSION in docker-publish.yml
3. Fails if a newer version exists

Schedule: weekly (e.g. Monday 05:00). Failure visible on default branch CI — does not block PRs.

## Acceptance criteria

- Scheduled workflow exists and runs weekly
- Workflow fails when CST_VERSION in docker-publish.yml is behind latest release
- Workflow passes when pinned version matches latest
- No impact on PR CI

title: Enable Group A zizmor audits (known-vulnerable-actions, hardcoded-container-credentials, unredacted-secrets)
description:
## Why

These three zizmor audits have near-zero false positive risk and catch real security bugs. They were disabled only to keep initial scope focused (YAGNI). No investigation needed before enabling.

## What needs to be done

In .github/zizmor.yml, remove disable: true for:
- known-vulnerable-actions
- hardcoded-container-credentials
- unredacted-secrets

Run zizmor locally to verify no existing findings. Fix any findings before committing.

## Acceptance criteria

- Three audits enabled in zizmor.yml
- pinning-lint CI passes with no new findings
- No regressions in existing workflow files

title: Expand zizmor audit coverage beyond pinning-only
description:
## Why

zizmor.yml currently disables all audits except unpinned-uses (YAGNI at initial scope). The project has grown: artifact-based builds, fork PR handling, elevated permissions jobs. Additional audits are now warranted.

## What needs to be done

Three tracks:
1. Enable Group A audits immediately (known-vulnerable-actions, hardcoded-container-credentials, unredacted-secrets)
2. Enable Group B audits after fork-safe-docker-ci merges (artipacked, cache-poisoning, excessive-permissions, template-injection, dangerous-triggers, impostor-commit)
3. Investigate Group C audits and decide per-item (self-hosted-runner, unpinned-images, unpinned-tools, use-trusted-publishing, job-container-escape)

## Acceptance criteria

- All zizmor audits have an explicit enable/disable decision with rationale documented
- No audit remains disabled purely by YAGNI default without re-evaluation

title: Document zizmor.yml allow-list requirement in ADR-002
description:
## Why

ADR-002 describes the two-tier pinning policy and the GitHub repo allow-list (Option G) but does not mention that zizmor.yml must also be updated when adding a new GitHub Action. Contributors hitting lint failures may fix the SHA pin but not know zizmor.yml is a separate gatekeeping mechanism.

## What needs to be done

- Add a 'Adding a new GitHub Action' checklist section to ADR-002 covering:
  1. Pin to SHA (Tier A) or use trusted exception (Tier B)
  2. Add to .github/zizmor.yml allow-list
  3. If forking: add to GitHub repo allow-list (Option G)
- Clarify the relationship between the three mechanisms and why each exists

## Acceptance criteria

- ADR-002 has a checklist section for adding a new GitHub Action
- All three gatekeeping mechanisms are documented with rationale
- No contributor should need to reverse-engineer zizmor.yml from a lint failure

title: Move CI section from README.md to CONTRIBUTING.md
description:
## Why

README.md has a CI section (added during fork-safe-docker-ci work) that covers the pinning-lint workflow. Developer-facing documentation belongs in CONTRIBUTING.md per project convention. CONTRIBUTING.md already has a CI/CD Pipeline Security section referencing ADR-002.

## What needs to be done

- Move workflow file path reference from README.md CI section into CONTRIBUTING.md CI/CD Pipeline Security section
- Remove CI section from README.md
- Ensure no content is lost (workflow file link currently only in README.md)

## Acceptance criteria

- README.md contains no CI section
- CONTRIBUTING.md CI/CD Pipeline Security section includes the pinning-lint.yml workflow file link
- No duplicate content between the two files

title: Task 2: Introduce pinact or ratchet for contributor-side SHA resolution
description:
Contributor-side helper to make ADR-002 Tier A SHA pinning low-friction. Tool rewrites 'uses: owner/action@vX' to 'uses: owner/action@<sha> # vX' on demand.

Scope:
- Pick one of pinact / ratchet (both OSS, compatible with D5 no-recurring-cost driver)
- Install / document for contributor use
- Optional pre-commit hook
- Document invocation in AGENTS.md or CONTRIBUTING.md

Out of scope:
- CI-side lint that BLOCKS PRs — tracked separately as v2u (the same tool likely covers both modes: pinact run / pinact check). Cross-link to v2u in implementation PR.

Tool selection criteria (carry from v2u):
- OSS / free
- Active project (commit cadence, release recency, maintainer count, no archived flag, low CVE history) — assess via GitHub MCP or gh CLI
- Cross-platform (Linux + macOS; Windows nice-to-have)
- Reasonable allowlist mechanism for Tier B actions

Acceptance:
- Tool selected with documented health-check evidence
- Install path documented (script or one-liner)
- Optional pre-commit hook config provided
- Cross-reference to v2u in PR

Dependency: blocked by Finding 4 (wkf) — if F4 re-evaluation shifts recommendation away from per-action SHA pinning (unlikely), this task becomes moot.

title: Finding 7b: Mandate Confirmation criterion + Revisit Triggers in create-adr skill
description:
Split from put (now closed). Skill-side half of original Finding 7.

The create-adr skill (ai-agent-workspace/.claude/commands/create-adr.md) does not currently mandate:
- a testable Confirmation criterion (MADR optional, not enforced by the skill)
- explicit Revisit Triggers

Add both as mandatory stages so future ADRs are forced to write them.

Note: this is separate from Finding 6 (581), which fixes mitigation positioning. Both are skill-side ADRs in the same skill file; coordinate the PRs.

Acceptance:
- Skill mandates a Confirmation stage requiring a testable criterion
- Skill mandates a Revisit Triggers stage (or equivalent)
- Skill committed via ai: prefix in ai-agent-workspace (not this repo)

Scope note: this finding's implementation is in ai-agent-workspace; tracking here because ADR-002 surfaced the gap.

title: Finding 6: Update create-adr skill so mitigations are considered before the recommendation
description:
The create-adr skill (ai-agent-workspace/.claude/commands/create-adr.md) currently has these stages in order:
  Stage 4 — add common alternatives
  Stage 5 — mitigate negative consequences
  Stage 6 — verify decision drivers
  Stage 7 — complete the ADR draft
  Stage 8 — restructure for readability
  Stage 9 — final review

By the time mitigations are added (Stage 5), the recommendation has effectively already been made in Stage 3 (draft). Mitigations end up as per-option apologetics that do not feed back into the option-selection logic.

Symptom observed during ADR-002 review: critic finding C-6 (asymmetric option treatment — chosen option mitigated, rejected options loaded with unmitigated negatives) and the need for Finding 4 (re-evaluate recommendation against mitigations).

Fix the skill so the recommendation is provisional until after mitigations and decision drivers are integrated. Provisional re-ordering:

  Stage 3.5: provisional recommendation (draft, flagged as such)
  Stage 4: alternatives + mitigations for each, including for the provisional recommendation
  Stage 5: decision drivers (with priorities)
  Stage 6: re-evaluate the provisional recommendation against (a) all options' mitigations and (b) the priority-ranked drivers; finalize or shift recommendation
  Stage 7: consequences and follow-up
  Stage 8: restructure
  Stage 9: final review

Acceptance:
- create-adr skill updated with re-ordered stages
- Stage 6 explicitly requires re-walking the option matrix with mitigations included
- Skill committed via ai: prefix in the ai-agent-workspace repo (not this repo)

Scope note: this finding applies to a skill that lives outside this repository. Implementation is in ai-agent-workspace; tracking the finding here is appropriate because ADR-002 surfaced the gap and this repo's ADR practice will benefit.

title: One-time review: rule files consistency (AGENTS.md ↔ constitution.md ↔ skills)
description: Audit rule files for drift. Verify AGENTS.md, constitution.md, and skill signatures (molecule-testing, technical-coach, review-documentation-here, format-markdown) stay in sync. Check: new principles added in constitution but missing from AGENTS.md instructions; skills listed in AGENTS.md that have been removed; skill frontmatter descriptions diverging from AGENTS.md references.
notes: One-time review, not a recurring gate. User will decide if/when to repeat.
close_reason: recreating as epic to support blocking relationship within 86r epic

title: Windows: nvm not recognized after install — Node.js LTS install fails
description:
## Problem

Installing Node.js LTS via nvm fails on Windows. nvm is not recognized as a command despite being installed via Chocolatey.

## Error

Host: moria, task: Install Nodejs LTS

```
nvm : The term 'nvm' is not recognized as the name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or
operable program. Check the spelling of the name, or if a path was included, verify that the path
is correct and try again.
At line:1 char:65
+ ... le]::InputEncoding = New-Object Text.UTF8Encoding $false; nvm install ...
+                                                               ~~~
    + CategoryInfo          : ObjectNotFound: (nvm:String) [], CommandNotFoundException
    + FullyQualifiedErrorId : CommandNotFoundException
```

rc: 1

## Root Cause (hypothesis)

nvm installed via Chocolatey but PATH not refreshed in the current Ansible session. Windows requires a new shell session or explicit PATH reload after Chocolatey installs.

## Workaround

Disable the entire role `win_ai_agent` — not needed for current scope.

## Acceptance Criteria

- nvm is recognized in the same Ansible session after install
- `nvm install lts` completes successfully without manual intervention

title: Windows Bug Fixes and Improvements
description:
## Purpose

Umbrella epic for all Windows provisioning bugs and improvements discovered during hands-on sessions.

## Scope

Tracks issues found while manually provisioning and using the Windows (moria) host on AWS, including bugs, workarounds, and follow-up documentation tasks.

title: Windows provisioning fails: Chocolatey install denied (Win32ErrorCode 5)
description:
## Problem

Windows provisioning fails when installing Chocolatey due to missing permissions.

## Error

Host: moria

```
- name: Install Chocolatey
     ^ column 3

fatal: [moria]: FAILED! => {
    "changed": false,
    "cmd": "Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process -Force\n[System.Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol = [System.Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol -bor 3072\niex ((New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadString('https://community.chocolatey.org/install.ps1'))",
    "msg": "Failed to run: '': Exception calling \"CreateProcess\" with \"7\" argument(s): \"CreateProcessW() failed (Access is denied, Win32ErrorCode 5 - 0x00000005)\"",
    "rc": 5
}
```

## Root Cause (hypothesis)

Ansible is not running with Administrator privileges on the Windows target. `CreateProcessW` Win32ErrorCode 5 = Access Denied — process spawning blocked by insufficient permissions.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Chocolatey installs successfully on moria without manual intervention
- Role validates or ensures elevated privileges before running install script
notes:
## Manual Workaround (resolved)

### Steps
1. Log in as Administrator on the Windows machine and reboot
2. After reboot, retry provisioning:
   ```bash
   ansible-playbook ./provision.yml --extra-vars "provider=aws platform=windows" -vvv >provision-aws-windows.log 2>&1
   ```

### Outcome
Problem resolved after step 1 (reboot as Administrator).

### AWS Note: Checking server readiness after reboot
Ping does NOT work on AWS Windows machines. Use nmap with -Pn to skip host discovery:
```bash
sudo nmap -v -sS -Pn -p 3389 $IPV4_ADDRESS
```
Port 3389 (RDP) open = machine ready. This applies to AWS machines in general.

title: Evaluate version-update mechanism: separation, Python rewrite, or external tool
description:
**Problem:** Current Ansible implementation has test coverage gaps, gets complex when adding tools, is fragile, hard to test, hard to extend. Open questions on structural direction before further investment.

**Why:** Implementation getting complex and fragile. Risk wasting effort on Ansible-based fixes (ypm, 2fr, g0k) if a different foundation is chosen.

**Questions to evaluate (merged from 48h):**
1. Should version check/upgrade tasks move to tool-specific subfolders for separation of concerns?
2. Should the mechanism be rewritten in Python with a plugin architecture to make adding new tools easier?
3. Is Renovate or another existing dependency-update tool a better fit than custom Ansible playbooks?

**Value:** Picking the right foundation enables easier testing, clearer logic, simpler extension, better error handling.

**Decision required:** Pick direction (keep Ansible refactor / Python rewrite / external tool) before making Ansible-based changes that might become obsolete.

**Note:** This must be resolved before ypm, 2fr, g0k to avoid wasted effort. Merged from 48h on 2026-05-13.

title: Eliminate duplication between query-versions.yml and perform-updates.yml
description:
**Problem:** Duplication between playbooks/update-versions/query-versions.yml and perform-updates.yml causes changes needed in multiple files, risks inconsistency, and obscures source of truth.

**Why:** Future maintainability, contradicts 'no duplication' principle.

**Value:** Single source of truth, easier updates, clearer intent, easier to understand and less confusion.

**Solution:** Extract common patterns from both playbooks into shared components or interfaces.

title: Refactor playbooks/tasks/query-versions.yml for tool-based grouping with interface pattern
description:
**Problem:** Current phase-based grouping causes scattered logic and requires changes across multiple sections when adding new tools.

**Why:** Improving future maintainability.

**Value:** Tool-based grouping with interface definition enables single point of extension, applying Open/Closed Principle, high cohesion and loose coupling — concepts easier to understand and implement.

**Solution:** Regroup query-versions.yml by tool instead of phase. Each tool implements a common interface (read pins, extract pins, fetch upstream, compare and report, fail if stale). Encapsulate tool-specific steps as separate tasklists.

title: Extend new-role.sh for version-update pattern consideration
description:
**Problem:** New roles risk installing tools at outdated versions, introducing security risks.

**Why:** Improving consistency for future role creation.

**Value:** Avoid forgetting to consider version updates when creating new roles.

**Solution:** Extend new-role.sh script and templates to incorporate playbooks/update-versions concept so new roles bake in version-update tasks from creation.

title: Finding: constitution/role checklist must mandate registering new tools in version-update playbooks
description: When a new role is created that installs a versioned tool, there is currently no rule requiring the author to add that tool to the version-update mechanism (fetch task file, query-versions.yml, perform-updates.yml, concept doc). This gap means new tools silently escape version tracking. The constitution or a role-creation checklist must mandate: for every new role that pins a version in defaults/main.yml, a corresponding fetch task and playbook wiring must be added.

title: Finding: document maintenance-playbook exception to Principle II in constitution
description: The justified exception allowing maintenance playbooks (procedural localhost operator tools) to contain inline logic rather than wrapping roles is only recorded in per-feature plan.md Complexity Tracking tables. It should be canonically stated in the constitution under Principle II so future agents apply it consistently. Name the pattern explicitly and reference playbooks/backup/, playbooks/restore/, playbooks/update-versions/ as precedents. Discuss exact wording when fixing.

title: Finding: SDKMAN query hardcoded to linuxx64, may miss arm64 availability
description: fetch-java-version.yml queries https://api.sdkman.io/2/candidates/java/linuxx64/versions/all. Temurin version strings are typically arch-agnostic in SDKMAN, but availability can diverge between amd64 and arm64. First step: verify whether a linuxarm64 endpoint exists in the SDKMAN API. If yes: query both and take intersection to find highest version available on all target architectures (amd64 hobbiton/rivendell, arm64 lorien). If no: keep current impl but document the limitation in docs/architecture/version-update-playbooks.md.

title: Finding: all tools need explicit major version pin
description: Only Java enforces same-major-patch tracking via java_sdkman_major. Flutter, gitmux, Nerd Fonts, and Android have no major version pin in role defaults. A major upstream bump passes silently through both playbooks. Each tool needs a pinned major variable in defaults and query-versions.yml should detect major-boundary crossings with a distinct error, separate from normal staleness.

title: Configure major version pinning for all tracked tools
description: Currently only Java pins major version explicitly (java_sdkman_major derived from java_sdkman_identifier). Other tools (Flutter, gitmux, Nerd Fonts, Android) have no major version pin, so a major version bump upstream would silently pass through query-versions.yml and perform-updates.yml without any warning. Need a mechanism to pin the expected major for each tool and detect when upstream crosses a major boundary, keeping major upgrades a deliberate manual decision.

title: Evaluate Renovate for automated tracking of pinned binary versions
description:
## Why

Binaries installed via curl in run: steps (e.g. container-structure-test) are invisible to Dependabot. Renovate supports a regex datasource that can match version strings in arbitrary files against upstream release feeds, enabling automated update PRs including SHA256 refresh.

## What needs to be done

- Evaluate Renovate regex datasource for tracking CST_VERSION and SHA256 env vars in docker-publish.yml
- Assess setup cost and maintenance overhead vs. the scheduled check workflow approach
- If viable, configure Renovate and retire the scheduled cst-version-check workflow

## Acceptance criteria

- Decision documented: adopt Renovate or explicitly keep scheduled check
- If adopted: Renovate opens PRs for CST updates with correct version and SHA256 values
- If rejected: rationale recorded here

title: Investigate and decide Group C zizmor audits
description:
## Why

These audits require project-specific investigation before enabling: they may be inapplicable, noisy, or require configuration.

## What needs to be done

- self-hosted-runner: is ubuntu-24.04-arm GitHub-hosted or self-hosted?
- unpinned-images: do any workflows use container: or services: blocks?
- unpinned-tools: any curl/pip installs beyond the now-pinned container-structure-test?
- use-trusted-publishing: conflicts with existing cosign/OIDC setup?
- job-container-escape: applicable given Docker image build jobs?

Update zizmor.yml with decisions. Document rationale for any remaining disable: true entries.

## Acceptance criteria

- Every Group C audit has an explicit decision with rationale in zizmor.yml comments
- No audit disabled purely by default without documented reason
- pinning-lint CI passes

title: Add rtk gain history to backup/restore playbooks
description:
## Why

rtk tracks token savings in ~/.local/share/rtk/history.db (SQLite, 336K). Without backup, gain history resets on every VM rebuild or machine switch.

## What needs to be done

Create two new playbooks and wire them into backup.yml / restore.yml, following the exact pattern of claude-settings.yml.

---

### 1. Create playbooks/backup/rtk-data.yml

```yaml
---
- name: Backup rtk data
  hosts: "{{ backup_from_host }}"
  tags: not-supported-on-vagrant-docker
  become: true

  vars:
    ansible_user: "{{ my_ansible_user }}"
    backup_user: "{{ desktop_users[0].name }}"

  tasks:
    - name: Backup rtk gain history
      include_tasks: backup.yml
      vars:
        path:
          - "/home/{{ backup_user }}/.local/share/rtk/history.db"
        backup_file: "rtk-backup.tar.gz"
```

### 2. Create playbooks/restore/rtk-data.yml

```yaml
---
- name: Restore rtk data
  hosts: linux
  tags: not-supported-on-vagrant-docker
  become: true

  vars:
    ansible_user: "{{ my_ansible_user }}"
    restore_user: "{{ desktop_users[0].name }}"

  tasks:
    - name: Restore rtk gain history
      include_tasks: restore.yml
      vars:
        users: "{{ [restore_user] }}"
        backup_file: "rtk-backup.tar.gz"
        destination_beneath_home: ".local/share/rtk"
        delete_before_beneath_home:
          - ".local/share/rtk/history.db"
```

### 3. Wire into backup.yml

Add to /home/galadriel/Documents/Cline/ansible-all-my-things/backup.yml under the Applications section:
```yaml
- import_playbook: playbooks/backup/rtk-data.yml
```

### 4. Wire into restore.yml

Add to /home/galadriel/Documents/Cline/ansible-all-my-things/restore.yml under the Applications section:
```yaml
- import_playbook: playbooks/restore/rtk-data.yml
```

---

## Key design decisions (do not deviate)

- Only history.db backed up — .device_salt intentionally excluded (machine-specific identity; restoring it corrupts telemetry fingerprinting on new hardware)
- delete_before_beneath_home scoped to history.db only — leaves .local/share/rtk/ dir intact so new machine's .device_salt survives the restore
- No exclusion_patterns needed — single file target
- Pattern source: playbooks/backup/claude-settings.yml and playbooks/restore/claude-settings.yml

## Reference files

- Pattern: playbooks/backup/claude-settings.yml, playbooks/restore/claude-settings.yml
- Shared tasks: playbooks/backup/backup.yml, playbooks/restore/restore.yml
- Entry points: backup.yml, restore.yml (project root)
acceptance_criteria:
- playbooks/backup/rtk-data.yml exists and matches the template above exactly
- playbooks/restore/rtk-data.yml exists and matches the template above exactly
- backup.yml imports rtk-data.yml under the Applications section
- restore.yml imports rtk-data.yml under the Applications section
- ansible-lint passes on all four modified/created files
- .device_salt is NOT in the backup path
- delete_before_beneath_home targets only history.db, not the whole rtk dir

title: Docs: AWS machines don't respond to ping — use nmap -Pn to check readiness
description:
## Why

AWS EC2 instances (including Windows) do not respond to ICMP ping. This trips up anyone waiting for a machine to be ready after boot or reboot.

Discovered while troubleshooting Windows provisioning (see ansible-all-my-things-dev Chocolatey bug).

## What to document

Add a note to the AWS docs explaining:
- Ping does NOT work on AWS machines
- Use nmap with -Pn (skip host discovery) to check reachability:
  ```shell
  # Windows (RDP port)
  sudo nmap -v -sS -Pn -p 3389 $IPV4_ADDRESS

  # Linux (SSH port)
  sudo nmap -v -sS -Pn -p 22 $IPV4_ADDRESS
  ```
- Port shown as 'open' = instance ready

## Where to add

- test/test_aws_provisioning.md — Step 1, after the provisioning commands (waiting for readiness context)
- docs/user-manual/prerequisites-aws.md — as operational tip

title: Fix deprecated Ansible usage pattern warnings in playbooks
description: When running playbooks, Ansible emits deprecation warnings about usage patterns that will break in future versions. These warnings should be identified and fixed across all playbooks to ensure forward compatibility.

value: AWS CLI v2 PyInstaller on ubuntu: COPY --from=builder /usr/local/bin/aws breaks the symlink chain. Bootloader uses $ORIGIN and looks for libpython3.14.so.1.0 in the binary's directory (/usr/local/bin/). Fix: use RUN ln -s /usr/local/aws-cli/v2/current/bin/aws /usr/local/bin/aws so the bootloader resolves the real dist/ path where the bundled library exists. gpg-agent must also be explicitly apt-installed in ubuntu (not auto-dep of gpg unlike Debian).

gh pr create in a fork repo defaults to upstream as the target repo. For cross-repo PR (fork branch → upstream main): use gh pr create --repo upstream/repo --head ForkOwner:branch --base main. Without --repo, gh uses the upstream remote which may not match the fork where commits live.
beads dep direction for epics: sub-tasks BLOCK the epic (bd dep add epic sub-task), not the other way. Wrong direction = sub-tasks never show as ready.
Branch names must allow identifying the associated epic easily. Convention: branch name should map to its parent epic so WIP can be inferred from git branch when no issues are in_progress.

scripts/test-molecule-all.sh runs molecule test for every role with a molecule/default/molecule.yml scenario, collects pass/fail results, and exits 1 if any failed. Use this in CI instead of reimplementing role discovery.
fetch-android-version.yml uses proximity regex ([\s\S]{0,400}?) to find SHA-1 near build number in HTML — fragile, tracked as TD-009, isolated per FR-007

Findings are always connected to WIP (in_progress or current branch). A finding not tied to active WIP must be converted to a normal feature request: headline must NOT contain the word 'Finding', though description may note provenance.

Per-arch Docker image tags prevent cross-arch manifest collision. When per-arch build jobs push to the same tag (e.g. :latest), last push wins and the other arch's test fails with 'no matching manifest for linux/amd64'. Fix: add arch-specific tags (latest-amd64, latest-arm64) via metadata-action type=raw,value=latest-${{ matrix.arch }}. Test jobs pull and test their own arch tag independently.
